title: WebKit renders the raycaster 4-5x slower than Chromium
symptom: In WebKit the idle scene alone costs ~23ms busy per frame (raycast-walls 19.1ms vs 4.2ms in Chromium, hud 4.2ms vs 0.7ms) — under 30fps before any combat happens; 336 slow frames in 3x30s vs 0 in Chromium.
culprit: The per-column pipeline (640 one-pixel-wide drawImage blits + fillRect overlays per frame) and per-frame fillText HUD are drastically more expensive in WebKit's canvas implementation. Caveat: measured in headless WebKit (possibly software-rendered); real Safari with GPU acceleration may be faster — needs one verification run on real hardware.
refactor: For WebKit (feature-detect), render walls into the same ImageData buffer the floor-caster already uses (pure typed-array writes + one putImageData) instead of per-column drawImage; cache static HUD text to an offscreen canvas. Alternatively: document Safari as degraded until verified on real hardware.

title: Minimap redraws every wall tile of the whole map, every frame
symptom: The hud phase grows with map area even when nothing on the minimap changed: 0.72ms on an 84x84 demo map vs 1.10ms on the 160x160 magento2 map (idle, minimap showing — the default HUD state). Extrapolates linearly: a 250x250 monster file costs ~2.5ms/frame just for the corner minimap.
culprit: renderMinimap's nested for-y/for-x loop issues one fillRect per wall tile over the entire grid every frame, plus per-category marker loops — O(map area) canvas calls for an image that only changes when doors/secrets open or entities move.
refactor: Render the static wall layer to an offscreen canvas once per level (invalidate on gridVersion bump — doors/secret walls already increment it), then per frame drawImage that cache + draw only the dynamic markers (player/enemies/pickups).
evidence: hud phase medians across s1 (84x84) vs s4 (160x160) baselines, 2026-07-18T00-13/09-05 runs
outcomeNote: FIXED 2026-07-18: walls cached to an offscreen canvas keyed on (map, gridVersion, cell). Measured: hud phase 0.72->0.10ms (demo), 1.09->0.24ms (160x160 magento2).

title: Wall-edge antialiasing is affordable — it could ship enabled
symptom: WALL_EDGE_ANTIALIASING_ENABLED is off by default awaiting cost data. Measured: no detectable busy-time cost on the demo scene (5.60 vs 5.70ms, below the 0.2ms detection floor) and +0.4ms/frame on the 160x160 magento2 map. No frame-interval regression in either.
culprit: The feared per-column extra fillRect + shadedTexel cost is real but small in Chromium; it scales with wall-column count, hence the modest magento2 delta.
refactor: Flip the flag on by default (Chromium/Firefox). If WebKit's canvas slowness (F23) is confirmed on real Safari, gate it off there via the same feature-detect.
evidence: Interleaved A/B, 5+5 runs per scenario, perf_runs/2026-07-18T01-01-33-870Z

title: Distance fog ('farther = darker') is free — keep it
symptom: User asked whether the fog effect slows rendering. Measured via a purpose-built DISTANCE_FOG_ENABLED A/B: fog-off saves under 0.1ms/frame on both the demo scene (4.90 vs 4.80) and the magento2 map (5.50 vs 5.40) — below the 0.2ms detection floor.
culprit: None — the 640 per-column alpha fillRects and the floor caster's per-pixel multiply batch efficiently in Chromium.
refactor: No action. Keep the flag (one-line toggle) for future per-browser measurements; it is also the mechanism that would cheaply confirm F23's attribution on real Safari.

title: pathField full-map reflood: hypothesis refuted at real map sizes
symptom: Static analysis flagged the O(map-area) BFS reflood on every player tile-crossing as a prime magento2 framedrop suspect. Measured with continuous noclip movement on the 160x160 map: busy median identical to standing still (5.50 vs 5.50ms); the sim phase rises only 0.11 -> 0.16ms.
culprit: The flat Int32Array BFS is simply fast — a 25.6k-tile reflood every ~150ms amortizes to ~0.05ms/frame. Preallocated buffers mean no GC pressure either.
refactor: None needed at current map scales. If maps ever grow past ~500x500, revisit with an incremental/dirty-region flood.

title: Console-sidebar DOM mirroring: no measurable frame cost at real log rates
symptom: Static analysis rated the per-console.log DOM append + scrollTop layout flush as a High combat-time cost. Measured (sustained fire on the 280-enemy map, sidebar active vs bypassed): busy p95/p99/max and heap slopes are indistinguishable across 5+5 interleaved runs.
culprit: Real combat emits a few logs per second, not the tens-per-frame the static estimate assumed; at that rate the layout flush is noise.
refactor: Optional hygiene: defer the scrollTop assignment to one rAF callback per batch of appends. Worth doing only if a future feature raises log frequency by an order of magnitude.

title: Constant per-frame allocation churn (buildStats, captureSnapshot, gamepad copy)
symptom: Every frame allocates: a ~20-field computeScore input + ~10-field result + ownedWeapons Set spread (buildStats, even while paused), an 18-field input snapshot + filtered array (captureSnapshot — evaluated before the ?. can short-circuit, so it runs with no recorder attached), and an Array.from copy of the gamepad list. Measured heap churn during combat: ~0.3MB/s with periodic GC; no measurable frame-time effect in 30s captures on this 32GB machine.
culprit: Argument evaluation before optional-chaining (engine.ts:1042), unmemoized computeScore call in buildStats (engine.ts:2322), Array.from(getGamepads()) (input.ts:428).
refactor: Three cheap, safe fixes: guard the record call with if (this.replayRecorder), memoize computeScore on its input signature, iterate the gamepad list without copying. Primarily lowers GC pressure on memory-constrained machines — the plausible profile of the Task 241 reporter's environment.
evidence: Static audit + heap slopes in s4 fire runs; 10-min soak in perf_runs (2026-07-18, s2 600s run)
outcomeNote: PARTIALLY FIXED 2026-07-18: captureSnapshot now guarded (only allocates with a recorder attached), gamepad list no longer copied per frame. computeScore memoization dropped as inapplicable — levelTimeSec is a real input (speed bonus), the score legitimately changes every frame; remaining churn accepted (measured Low).

title: Replay viewer produces garbage ?perfDebug=1 phase logs
symptom: Watching a replay with ?perfDebug=1 logs monotonically growing phase times (seconds-scale 'busy' per frame) — any future perf capture taken during replay playback is unusable without external correction.
culprit: The replay rAF loop calls engine.advance() directly and never calls perf.beginFrame(), so FramePerfLogger's per-frame phase map is never reset.
refactor: Have the replay loop call a small public engine.beginPerfFrame(rawDtMs) wrapper (or fold beginFrame into advance() using a stashed rawDt). The benchmark harness currently works around it by differencing.
evidence: Discovered by the harness: S2 busy(med)=6955ms; verified against replay-loop code
outcomeNote: FIXED 2026-07-18: perf-frame begin moved into advance() itself (frame() hands over the unclamped delta; direct drivers fall back to their own dt) — replay viewer and headless drivers now produce correct ?perfDebug=1 phases.

title: Task 241 (magento2 120->30fps): not reproducible as engine cost — evidence points environmental
symptom: The reported drop could not be reproduced in any of 6 magento2 scenarios (idle / look / sustained fire / dry-fire / mousemove-flood / continuous movement, 160x160 map, 280 enemies): engine busy never exceeded ~13ms worst-frame, medians ~5.6ms. In headed desktop sessions, slow frames DID appear — but every single one was a missed vsync with ~27ms unaccounted and normal (~5ms) game phases, i.e. stalls outside game code; headless runs of identical scenarios show zero slow frames.
culprit: Most plausible: the reporter's compositor/GPU path (disabled hardware acceleration, large high-DPI upscale of the 640x400 canvas, 120Hz display doubling compositor deadlines), extensions, or memory-pressure GC — not raycaster/AI/pathfinding CPU. The mousemove-flood test also weakens the high-poll-mouse theory (no effect at ~2000 events/s).
refactor: Sharpen the ?perfDebug=1 capture request to the reporter: have them include the [perf] env: line and a few SLOW lines — if their SLOW frames show small phases + large unacct (matching our headed pattern), direct them to chrome://gpu (hardware acceleration), window size/DPR, and extensions rather than waiting on engine optimizations. The F7+F10 allocation fixes are the only engine-side lever that plausibly helps a memory-constrained machine.
evidence: All s4-* cells across perf_runs/2026-07-18T00-13 (headed) and 09-05..09-47 (headless); slow-frame phase attributions

title: RESPONSIVE_CANVAS_SCALING: no engine-side cost; compositor cost unverified
symptom: A/B shows zero busy-time difference (5.00 vs 5.00 idle; replay identical). The flag's real risk — compositor upscale cost of a larger on-screen canvas — is not observable headless, and the headed attempt was invalidated by desktop throttling.
culprit: Engine work is resolution-independent (fixed 640x400 internal canvas); only the browser's scale-and-composite step grows.
refactor: Safe to enable from the engine's perspective. Before shipping, do one attended headed spot-check (fps overlay on, window maximized) — the harness cell exists (--flag scaling), it just needs an unlocked screen.
outcomeNote: VERIFIED on-screen 2026-07-18 (user-attended headed A/B, 5+5 interleaved runs): busy median 6.70 vs 6.70ms, intervals/p95/slow-counts identical — compositor upscale is free at desktop window sizes. Safe to enable whenever the resizing behavior itself is wanted; perf is no longer a blocker.

title: Bundle and startup are healthy
symptom: Cold-cache first-interactive payload is ~17KB gzipped (HTML+CSS+main JS 101KB gz parses in ~200ms on mid-range hardware); all 18 tree-sitter wasm grammars (2.9MB gz total) and the 223KB default-highscore replay load lazily on first use. No render-blocking resources.
culprit: None — build-time-fetch + per-language lazy loading is working as designed.
refactor: Optional: code-split the replay system out of the main bundle (~10-15% startup parse win). Not recommended now — architectural churn for a page that is already fast.
evidence: dist/ size table + startup flow analysis (static audit D)
